data.name: Timeline
data.description: An educational and inspirational walkthrough that allows you to learn while you explore the collection.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
BIRTH OF GURU NANAK.    
Guru nanak, the founder of Sikhism, born in Nankana Sahib near Lahore into a Hindu merchant (Khatri) family of the Bedi clan.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE BIRTH OF GURU NANAK- Guru Nanak (1469-1539), the first of the ten Gurus of the Sikhs, was born in Nankana Sahib near Lahore to a Hindu merchant (Khatri) family and he amazed those around him with his spiritual insight from childhood. Nanak questioned the status quo, starting with the rituals the family priest wanted him to follow, his teacher's instructions and the injustices he saw in society. He rejected all distinctions of religion, caste, and gender – seeking solitude in devotion and serving the poor. The story of the Sachā Saudā, the True Investment, is emblematic of Nanak’s mindset. When Nanak was 18 years old, his father gave him twenty rupees to go to the city and buy some goods that he could trade to make a profitable investment. But the young Guru used that money to buy food and clothes for the poor. Upon returning home the Guru explained to his father that helping others was the True Investment. He institutionalized this sentiment later as langar, or free communal kitchens, that are present in Gurdwaras (Sikh Temple) worldwide

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE GURU'S MESSAGE OF UNITY.    
Guru Nanak declared “There is no Hindu, there is no Muslim”, after experiencing the Divine, emphasizing the unity of humanity.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE GURU'S MESSAGE OF UNITY - At the age of 27, Guru Nanak went missing when bathing in the Kali Bein River in the early hours of the day. Onlookers thought the Guru had drowned and a search commenced, but was fruitless. When Guru Nanak emerged after three days of meditation, he remained quiet for some time but eventually spoke these words, “There is no Hindu, there is no Muslim”. These were the two dominant religions of the time in Punjab and there was friction between them. Guru Nanak explained to the the people that we should look beyond superficial identities, treating everyone the same, and emphasizing the practice of virtuous action instead of becoming arrogant due to religious affiliation. God for Guru Nanak is formless and boundless and is the creator, the sustainer and the destroyer of the universe and we are all the same in his eyes. His concept of One God [Ek Onkar] was mirrored in his understanding of the unity within humanity which manifested in practice by serving all.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
GURU NANAK'S TRAVELS.  
Guru Nanak begins his lengthy travels, covering 28,000 kms (17,500 miles) over 24 years, across India, South Asia and the Middle East.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: GURU NANAK'S TRAVELS - At the age of 31 Guru Nanak set off on a series of four long journeys (Udāsīs) lasting 24 years, taking with him his musical companion Mardana and on some his devotee Bala. Guru Nanak traveled over 28,000 kms. (17,500 miles) over these 24 years, visiting religious pilgrimage sites and festivals across India, South Asia and Arabia, preaching the core principles of Sikhism. The group reached sites in and around Mecca towards the West, up to Ladakh in the north, as far East as Assam, and as far south as Sri Lanka. Guru Nanak planted the seed of Sikhi in all these regions, and to this day communities of Sikhs can be found across the world. The Guru also engaged in interfaith dialogue with thought leaders of Hindu, Muslim, Buddhist, Jain, Yogi, and Sufi saints, to learn from and also share his message of One God [Ekonkar] who is beyond form, omnipresent, everlasting, formless, boundless and beyond description.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
GOD IS OMNIPRESENT.  
Guru Nanak arrives in Mecca, visiting the Kabba, preaching about God’s omnipresence and the importance of virtuous action.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: GOD IS OMNIPRESENT - During Guru Nanak’s extensive travels, he reached Mecca, the holiest of sites for the Muslim community. On arriving at the central shrine, known as the Kabba and tierd from the long journey, Guru Nanak decided to rest. As he lay down, his feet were pointed towards the Kabba. This upset the local priest, who asked him to move his feet away from the direction of God. To this the Guru replied, “Point my feet in a direction where there is no God” – the shocked priest realized his folly. Guru Nanak’s concise yet profound response points to his vision of God [Vahiguru] as omnipresent – in every place, at all times – while also stressing the formlessness of God [Vahiguru]. Guru Nanak's eternal, omnipresent, and formless God pervades all trees, mountains, and living beings equally. His light is in everything and everyone, including you and me.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
KARTARPUR ESTABLISHED.  
Guru Nanak settles a new city named Kartarpur, where a free communal kitchen (langar) was established and community would gather for worship.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: KARTARPUR ESTABLISHED - After Guru Nanak’s lengthy travels, at the age of 55, the Guru settled and established the town of Kartarpur, meaning the City of the Creator, alongside the river Ravi, north of the city of Amritsar. Here Guru Nanak organized a free communal kitchen (langar), which served people who sat shoulder the shoulder irrespective of their religion, caste, gender, and creed. The langar served to foster a feeling of equality amongst the Guru's followers, and also to help overcome caste prejudices that traditionally prohibited the higher and lower casts from cooking, sharing and eating food together. A community (sangat) was gathered which united for service (sevā), devotional worship (bhakti), spiritual discussion (goshṭī), and hard work (kirat kamāī). Guru Nanak’s tradition was built on the three pillars of, honest work (kirat karo), sharing one’s earnings (vand chako), and devotional worship (Naam Japo).

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE SECOND GURU.  
Guru Angad (1504-1552) is anointed as Guru, and he formalizing the Gurmukhi script and commissioning the life stories of Guru Nanak, called the Janamsakhis.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE SECOND GURU - Guru Angad (1504-1552), born with the name Lehna, was a devout follower of the Goddess Durga before a chance meeting with Guru Nanak in Kartarpur. This meeting changed Lehna’s life – in meeting Guru Nanak he had quenched his spiritual thirst. Lehna served Guru Nanak for years, passing all the spiritual tests that came his way. Guru Nanak renamed Lehna as Angad, meaning “limb”, symbolizing their spiritual unity. As second Guru of the Sikhs, Guru Angad innovated and promoted the Gurmukhi script, which gave the Sikh community a distinct script, just like Hindus have the Devanagari script and Muslims the Arabic script. The Guru assembled all the writings of Guru Nanak and commissioned the life stories (janamsākhīān) of Guru Nanak. This endeavour sowed the seed for the histories of Guru Nanak to be written and many different life stories (janamsākhīāni) exist, including the Bala, Puratan, and Meharban versions.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE THIRD GURU.   
Guru Amardas (1479-1574) anointed as Guru, who established the Sikh community at Goindwal, preaching for the equality of all, and writing devotional poetry.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE THIRD GURU - Guru Amardas (1479-1574), the third Sikh Guru, was a seeker of the Divine [Vahiguru] for much of his life but this journey was forever changed when hearing the melodious singing of his daughter in law (nephew’s wife), Bibi Amaro, who directed him to Guru Angad. Soon thereafter Guru Amardas became a disciple of Guru Angad, despite being older. After nearly twenty years of service, he was appointed Guru at the age of 73. Guru Amardas established the city of Goindwal, built a stepwell (Baoli Sahib), and appointed regional leaders, and strengthened the Sikh community by organizing biannual gatherings twice a year, in the spring for Vaisakhi and fall for Diwali. A prolific poet, the Guru composed an extensive amount of Gurbani. He served as Guru for nearly two decades before passing the title to his son-in-law, Guru Ramdas.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE FOURTH GURU.   
Guru Ramdas (1534-1581) anointed as Guru – was the architect and founder of the city of Amritsar, including its sacred pond (sarovar).
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE FORTH GURU - Guru Ramdas (1534-1581), the fourth Sikh Guru, was born in Lahore and became the son-in-law of Guru Amardas. From a young age, he was a steadfast disciple of the Guru, entrusted with key administrative and communal responsibilities. As Guru, he founded and developed Amritsar (previously called Guru Ka Chakk or Ramdaspur), himself digging the sacred pool (sarovar) in which Sikhs would bathe for its medicinal properties. The Guru was also a prolific poet, composing many commonly read prayers like the Lāvān – the verses recited at Sikh weddings – as well poetry written in over thirty musical modes (rāgas). Although he was only Guru for seven years, he firmly planted the Sikh community in Amritsar – which would remain the heart of Sikhism to this day.  Before passing he appointed his young son, Arjan, as Guru.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
ROYAL PATRONAGE.  
Akbar (1542-1605), the most tolerant of the Mughal Emperors, (reign 1556-1605), granted land for the Guru’s use where Amritsar is now located.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: ROYAL PATRONAGE - Akbar, becomes the third Mughal Emperor and was known as the most tolerant of the Mughals, bringing forth a long period of peace and stability in the region. The history of the Mughal Empire and Sikh Guru period intertwined as they overlapped from the 16th century to the 18th century. The Mughal Empire began with Babur (1483-1530), who was criticized for his brutality during the conquest by Guru Nanak in his Bābur Bāṇī (GGS: 722). Akbar however had a pluralistic approach, coined as ‘suhl-i-kul’, peace for all, demonstrated by his removal of the tax (jizya) on non-Muslims and incorporation of Rajputs into his ruling structure. It was during this time when Akbar met with Guru Amardas, Guru Ramdas, and Guru Arjan Dev Ji. Sikh history records that Emperor Akbar donated the land of Amritsar to the Sikh community. The Sikh Guru Amardas, also petitioned Akbar to prohibit unjust practices like sati, where a widow would be forced to join her dead husband on his funeral pyre.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE FIFTH GURU.  
Guru Arjan (1563-1606) anointed Guru – builds the Harimandar in Amritsar and assembles the Adi Granth, a compilation of the Gurus’ writings along with other saints..
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE FIFTH GURU - Guru Arjan (1563-1606), the fifth Guru, was the youngest son of Guru Ramdas. To preserve the authenticity of Guru Nanak’s message and writings, he compiled all Sikh scripture related to his predecessor Gurus into the Adi Granth, which was completed in 1604. To do this he gathered all the writings of earlier Gurus and added some writings of revered Hindu saints such as Bhagat Kabir, Ramananda, Namdev, Ravidas, and Muslim sufi saint Sheik Farid. The group included twenty eight other Bhagats, Bhatts, Pirs and Gursikhs. The Sikh scripture is globally unique in that it contains thinkers of other religions, like Hinduism and Islam, finding commonality to encourage unity. Besides compiling the text, the Guru was also an exceptional poet, writing more poetry than any other composer within the Adi Granth. Guru Arjan also established the city of Tarn Taran, 25 km (15 miles) south of Amritsar.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
GOLDEN TEMPLE BUILT.    
Harimandar Sahib, also known the Golden Temple, completed in 1601. The Adi Granth was installed within the temple in 1604.
timelineGalleries.longDescription:
Harmandir Sahib, meaning the Temple of God (Hari), was completed in 1601 under the guidance of Guru Arjan, along with Baba Budha and Bhai Salo. Taking nearly twenty years to build, it established Amritsar as the spiritual center of the Sikh community, a role it continues to hold today. The shrine was designed like a white lotus floating within the sacred pool, the Amrit Sarovar, symbolizing purity and spiritual awakening.

In 1604, Guru Arjan compiled and installed the Adi Granth within the temple. The scripture is revered as a living Guru, embodying divine wisdom equal to that of the Sikh Gurus. Each day begins with the parkash ceremony, where the Adi Granth is respectfully carried into the sanctum on a palanquin, and ends with the sukhasan, when it is taken to rest at the Akal Takht.

The shrine later became widely known as the Golden Temple after Maharaja Ranjit Singh adorned it with gold leaf in 1830.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
MARTYRDOM OF GURU ARJUN.     
Due to the growing popularity of Guru Arjan, Mughal Emperor Jahangir demands that he convert to Islam, when the Guru refuses, he is put to death.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: MARTYRDOM OF GURU ARJAN - Following the peaceful period of rule of Mughal Emperor Akbar, his son Jahangir took the throne in 1605. The political and religious camps that supported Jahangir pushed him to take extremist positions upon ascending to the throne, and one of his first acts was to arrest the fifth Guru, Guru Arjan (1563-1605), whose increasing popularity threatened the Mughals. Jahangir first tried to convert Guru Arjan, bringing him under the fold of Islam, but Guru Arjan vehemently declined, even though it meant sacrificing his life. The Guru willfully withstood days of torture before passing away. This atrocious act by the Mughal government transformed the peaceful Sikh community into a martial group, concerned for their survival. The next Guru, Guru Hargobind (1595-1644), militarized the community, establishing a fighting force called the Akal Sena, to defend the community and combat injustice.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE SIXTH GURU. 
Guru Hargobind (1595-1644), militarized the Sikh community, fighting back against the oppressive Mughal government.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE SIXTH GURU. Following his father's martyrdom, Guru Hargobind (1595-1644) was anointed as the sixth Guru, given a mission to militarize and defend the Sikh community under threat from the Mughal government. During his tenure he would establish several new cities, namely Hargobindpur and Kiratpur, while successfully fending off four major attacks from the Mughals. Guru Hargobind himself fought in these battles and was known for his large physique, some accounts suggest he was nearly seven feet tall. The battles included the Battle of Amritsar, Gurusar, Hargobindpur, and finally Kartarpur (Doaba). The last battle was the largest, in which over 50,000 Mughal soldiers were defeated by Sikh forces. Adorning two swords, one representing mīrī, sovereignty, and the other pīrī, spirituality, the Guru encouraged his Sikhs not to ignore socio-political issues. Guru Hargobind’s tenure of guru was the lengthiest second only to Guru Nanak.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE TIMELESS THRONE.  
The Akal Takat (Throne of the Timeless) is established in Amritsar as an institution where temporal matters related to the community could be discussed.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE TIMELESS THRONE - The Akal Takht, the Throne of the Timeless, was constructed by Guru Hargobind (1595-1644), as a center for temporal ( political and worldly) authority of the Sikhs. The Guru would sit here with his warriors, statsmen, and devotees during the afternoon time to discuss politics and listen to heroic ballads (ḍhāḍhī vārān). The Akal Takht has held great importance for the Sikh community, and proclamations made from the Throne would carry significant weight. The collective community of the Khalsa would gather at the Akal Takht to discuss military and political strategy during the chaotic eighteenth century. Even during the reign of Maharaja Ranjit Singh, the command of the Akal Takht was held with such reverence, that when Ranjit Singh was summoned, he graciously accepted punishment for his conduct which was unbecoming of a Sikh.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE SEVENTH GURU.   
Guru Harrai (1630-1661) is anointed Guru. An apt statesman, the Guru keeps peace during a political instable period while retaining his cavalry force.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE SEVENTH GURU - Guru Harrai (1630-1661), the seventh Guru of the Sikhs, was the grandson of Guru Hargobind and was anointed Guru at the age of fifteen. Based in the Punjab Hills, in the city of Kiratpur, he maintained the Akal Sena, the standing army of 2,200 salarymen, established by his grandfather. Guru Harrai had a fondness for nature, and would often hold spiritual gatherings in forests, where kirtan, devotional singing of Gurbani, and scripture discourse, katha, would be performed. Guru Harrai was an excellent statesman, and balanced political tensions with the Mughals while addressing internal conflict within the Sikh community. Guru Harrai served as Guru for seventeen years, spreading the importance of respecting, reading, and understanding the meanings of Gurbani.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE EIGHTH GURU.   
Guru Harkrishan (1656-1664) anointed as Guru. At a young age he died of smallpox, visiting those infected to comfort them during their illness.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE EIGHTH GURU - Guru Harkrishan (1656-1664), the eight Guru of the Sikhs, was anointed at the young age of five due to his profound depth apparent at this tender age. Born in Kiratpur to Guru Harrai and Mata Krishan Devi, the young Guru won the admiration of Rajput rulers and common people alike. Guru Harkrishan is remembered for bestowing spiritual insight and coming to the aid of those with the deadly contagious disease of smallpox. The Guru served selfishly, ultimately contracting the disease himself. Remembered as a healer and saint, he passed away at the age of eight after gesturing his next successor should be his great uncle, Guru Tegh Bahadur. Bangla Sahib Gurdwara in Delhi marks the spot where Guru Harkrishan lived his last days.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE NINTH GURU.   
Guru Tegh Bahadur (1621-1675) anointed Guru, traveling extensively throughout South Asia while writing poetry which would be included in the Guru Granth Sahib.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE NINTH GURU - Guru Tegh Bahadur (1621-1675), the ninth Guru, was the youngest son of the sixth Guru, Guru Hargobind (1595-1644). Tegh Bahadur earned his stripes training in martial arts, fighting in the Battle of Kartarpur (1635), at the young age of fourteen. At the same time, Guru Tegh Bahadur was known for his deep detachment and meditative nature, often spending long periods of time in contemplation. Anointed as Guru in 1664, Guru Tegh Bahadur was the second most traveled Guru, spreading the message of Sikhi and providing to the poor. Guru Tegh Bahadur traveled as far east as Assam before settling the town of Anandpur, originally named Chakk Nanaki, named after his mother.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
MARTYRDOM OF GURU TEGH BAHADUR.    
Guru Tegh Bahadur, advocating for religious freedom, was executed in Delhi by the orders of the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: MARTYRDOM OF GURU TEGH BAHADUR - Guru Tegh Bahadur’s (1621-1675) widespread appeal caught the attention of Hindu Pandits from Kashmir, who arrived requesting support against religious conversation. In endeavoring to protect the Pandits' right to religious practice, the Guru was later summoned by Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb to Delhi. The emperor and his ministers schemed that if the Guru were to convert to Islam this would result in other Hindus also converting. Guru Tegh Bahadur however remained steadfast and did not convert, even enduring through great torture on behalf of the Pandits, before accepting death face on. The sacrifice of Guru Tegh Bahadur for the rights of other’s religious practice is an extraordinary feat unparalleled to this day in world history.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE TENTH GURU.   
Guru Gobind Singh (1666-1708) anointed Guru. A poet, philosopher and a warrior, militarized the Sikh’s further to defend the population from oppression.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE TENTH GURU - Guru Gobind Singh (1666-1708), the tenth Guru, was the only son of Guru Tegh Bahadur (1621-1675). From a young age the Guru was trained in Punjabi, Persian, and Sanskrit – along with martial arts. A gifted poet and patron of the arts, he composed spiritually rich and martial-spirted Gurbani, including compositions which are recited daily by Sikhs – Jāp, Svaiyye, Chaupaī. These compositions can be found in the Dasam Granth, which is a collection of the Guru's writings. Guru Gobind Singh expanded the militarization of the Sikhs, creating the Khalsa, a brotherhood of martial Sikhs, marked by their five symbols of faith (five k’s). Guru Gobind Singh fought in nearly two dozen battles while also finalizing the Guru Granth Sahib, and prior to  his passing in 1708, he anointing the scripture as the eternal Guru for Sikhs.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE KHALSA .   
The Khalsa, a brotherhood of martial Sikhs, was created by Guru Gobind Singh with the five K’s established as their symbols.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: Khalsa is the martial brotherhood created by Guru Gobind Singh on Vaisakhi Day in 1699 in Anandpur Sahib, initiating the five beloved ones (punj piāre) through the intiation ceremony called khande-kī-pāhul. This martial community would adopt five symbols of their faith, namely 1 the unshorn hair (kesh), a symbol of martial prowess & spirituality, 2 long underwear (kacha), a symbol of chastity & restraint, 3 a sword (kirpan), a reminder to uphold justice & to fight tyranny, 4 an iron bangle (kara), a symbol to pursue virtuous action, 5 the comb (kanga), a symbol & practice tool to keep clean & integrated with the world. The Khalsa’s mission was to serve humanity, fighting against injustice & protecting the marginalized. Male initiates were given the last name Singh, meaning lion or tiger, imbuing them with a martial ethos. Women were given the last name Kaur, meaning princess, uplifting the status of Sikh women. The uniform adoption of last names was aimed at creating communal bonds.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
MARTYRDOM OF OLDER PRINCES.   
The eldest two sons of Guru Gobind Singh obtain martyrdom fighting heroically against the injust Mughal army at the Fort of Chamkaur.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: MARTYRDOM OF OLDER PRINCES - Following the siege on Anandpur Sahib in 1704 by Mughal and Rajput forces, the Sikh community had to evacuate the city. Guru Gobind Singh along with forty Sikh warriors, later found refuge in the fort of Chamkaur, which was eventually surrounded by Mughal forces. During this battle, the two eldest sons of Guru Gobind Singh, Ajit Singh, aged 17 and Jujhar Singh, aged 13, along with the forty Sikh soldiers heroically fought, obtaining martyrdom (shahīdī) against a very large enemy force. Guru Gobind Singh writes about this battle in his letter to Aurangzeb, entitled, Zaffarnāmā, the Letter of Victory, a poetic letter written in classical Persian, which criticized the emperor's heavy handed brutality while proudly praising the feats of his warriors against an overwhelming large force.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: MARTYRDOM OF YOUNGER PRINCES.  The two younger sons of Guru Gobind Singh, aged 7 and 5, obtain martyrdom defiantly refusing to convert to Islam.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: MARTYRDOM OF YOUNGER PRINCES - During the Mughal attack on Anandpur Sahib in 1704, amongst the captured were Guru Gobind Singh’s two youngest sons, Zorawar Singh, aged 8, and Fateh Singh, aged 5 along with their Grandmother, Mata Gujri. The Mughal Governor of Sirhind, ordered the young boys to convert to Islam. The valiant princes (sāhibzāde) refused. Accounts describe the young princes bricked alive – as each layer of brick was stacked, they were asked again if they would convert, which they steadfastly refused. Their martyrdom (shahīdī) remains iconic in the annals of world history, such faith and determination was demonstrated at such a young age. A local merchant, Diwan Todar Mal, requested to perform the funeral rites but was denied, being told he would have to pay for the land to be used for cremation site by placing down gold coins over the entire parcel. Todar Mal then purchased what was the most expensive land ever, ensuring that the princes received their last rites.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
THE ETERNAL GURU.    
Guru Gobind Singh anoints the Guru Granth Sahib, the Sikh scripture, as the eternal Guru, to guide Sikhs following his passing.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE ETERNAL GURU - In 1708, prior to passing, Guru Gobind Singh (1666-1708) anointed the Guru Granth Sahib as the spiritual and eternal Guru of the Sikhs, ending the line of living Gurus. This version of the Guru Granth Sahib was finalized in 1704, adding the compositions of his father, Guru Tegh Bahadur, to the Adi Granth, compiled earlier by Guru Arjan Dev Ji. This was completed during the Guru’s stay at Damdama Sahib in Bathinda. Since that time Sikhs have sought spiritual guidance through reading, understanding, and singing the poetry, Gurbani. To read the Guru Granth Sahib is for a Sikh to have a conversation with the Guru on spiritual matters. As such, Sikhs treat the Guru Granth Sahib with the same respect and reverence that would be shown to a living Guru, waving a whisk (chaur) overtop and placing it on an elevated platform. To transport the scripture the Guru Granth Sahib is cereminiously wrapping it in ornate cloth (Rumala) and carried on the head as the scripture are recite

timelineGalleries.shortDescription:
KHALSA ARMY.    
Guru Gobind Singh bestows temporal (worldly and political) authority to the collective Khalsa community following his passing, instructing them to fight against injustice.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: KHALSA ARMY - Along with anointing the Guru Granth Sahib as Guru, which would inform Sikhs on spiritual matters, Guru Gobind Singh (1666-1708) anointed the collective Khalsa community as Guru for temporal (worldly and political) matters. This meant when the collective community had issues they would get together to discuss the path forward, all coming together under a single agreement – this would be called a Gurmatta, literally meaning the Guru's proclamation. The Khalsa was instructed to fight against injustice, wherever it may be, helping those who are marginalized, setting up communal kitchens (langar), while preaching and practising the Guru’s word (shabad). The Khalsa was distinctively known through their five symbols, the unshorn hair (kesh), sword (kirpan), iron bracelet (kara), underwear (kach), and comb (kanga).

timelines.name: WARRIOR SAINTS AND THE SIKH EMPIRE

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: WESTERN INVASIONS - Since the 8th century, invasions from Persian, central Asia, and Afghanistan have decimated North India, particularly Punjab. This was particularly true in the 18th century.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: WESTERN INVASIONS - Since the eighth century, invaders from the west, all traveling through Punjab. They came in waves over the next several centuries, bringing with them Islam, a new religion to India. These foreign invaders established various kingdoms, which expanded greatly in the twelfth century, even though they were a minority. With these new forms of government came periods of struggle and conflict, with religion sometimes being used as a tool of oppression on the locals. In the sixteenth century, Babur led a group called the Mughals, ethnically Turko-Mongol, who would establish rule across India for over two centuries. Persians (Nadir Shah) and Afghans (Ahmad Shah Abdali) both led invasions into India during the eighteenth century, causing great calamity. These invasions had a lasting impact on the Punjab, as it was the gateway into India from the West.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: KHALSA REVOLT - Banda Bahadur (1670-1716) leads a revolt in Punjab following the passing of Guru Gobind Singh, capturing the district of Sirhind.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: KHALSA REVOLT - Banda Bahadur (1670-1716) was one of the most impactful Sikhs of the 18th century. Originally a Bairāgī Sannyāsī renunciate, he was captivated by Guru Gobind Singh and initiated as a Sikh. He led a mission to avenge the unjust killing of Guru Gobind Singh’s young children and quickly capture swaths of land in Punjab. Banda’s group established Sikh rule in parts of Punjab and attracted the attention of the the entire Mughal army. In 1716, Banda Bahadur was captured, tortured, and killed in Delhi when refusing to convert to Islam, along with 700 Sikh warriors. The heroism of these Sikhs and Banda Bahadur continues to be remembered by Sikhs daily in their ardās prayer.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: MUGHAL INJUSTICE - Bhai Mani Singh (1644-1738), Sikh community leader and scholar, is executed by the Mughal Lahore Court under false pretexts.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: MUGHAL INJUSTICE - Bhai Mani Singh (1644-1738) the scribe and direct student of Guru Gobind Singh, reestablished the Sikh community at Amritsar, becoming the head priest (granthī) at the Golden Temple, Harimandar Sahib, following the Guru’s passing in 1708. Mani Singh also headed a seminary in which texts were produced on Sikh scripture and Sikh history. As he was the lead community organizer he was targeted, accused of withholding tax revenue from the Mughal government and the resulting dispute led to his arrest. Imbued with the Sikh spirit of defiance, not accepting external authority, he refused to convert to Islam and pay the tax. The government thought they could torture Mani Singh, and began chopping him joint by joint, but he bravely endured the torture, obtaining martrydom. His sacrifice, which sparked a fire under the Sikh Missals at the time, is still remembered today by Sikhs in their daily prayer (ardās) with the words- "those who were cut limb by limb" (band band kaṭāe).

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: THE SMALL AND LARGE MASSACRES Known as the small and large Ghallugharas (massacres), tens of thousands of Sikhs were killed during two violent campaigns by Mughal and Afghan forces.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: THE GHALLUGHARA'S - The eighteenth century was a period of great turmoil and triumph for Sikhs. Two great tragedies stand out, notably the Small and Large Massacres (ghallugharas). The 1746 ‘Small Massacre’ (chota ghallughara) was orchestrated by the Mughal governor of Lahore, who killed approximately 10,000 Sikhs. This was followed by the 1762 ‘Large Massacre’ (vadda ghallughara) by the Afghan, Ahmad Shah Abdali, who encircled largely Sikh noncombatants and killed nearly 50,000 men, women, and children, on a single day. Despite these tragic events, Sikhs fought with determination for justice, creating a kingdom in Punjab which enjoyed lasting peace for nearly half a century. Sikhs to this day remember the sacrifices made in the pursuit of justice and the common good.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: HEADLESS HEROICS - Afghan invader Ahmad Shah Abdali desecrated the Harmandir, Baba Deep Singh (1682-1757) obtains martyrdom (shahīdī) in this battle, toevict the Afgans.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: HEADLESS HEROICS - During the 1757 Afghan invasion of Punjab by the Ahmad Shah Abdali, a small force of Sikh warriors led by Baba Deep Singh (1682-1757), fought to the death, pushing out Afghan forces from Amritsar. A warrior-saint initiated by Guru Gobind Singh himself, Deep Singh spent much of his life teaching Sikh scripture and hand copying the Guru Granth Sahib. In his old age, when news arrived of the desecration of Harimandar Sahib, he took up arms once more, vowing to fight until his last breath. Though struck by a normally fatal blow to the neck, Sikh tradition recalls how that he continued, heroically fighting until reaching the sacred shrine. His sacrifice and determination have inspired generations of Sikhs.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: SIKH WOMEN - Birth of Sada Kaur, a seasoned general, she was the mentor and mother-in-law of Maharaja Ranjit Singh and the wife of Gurbaksh Singh, the leader of the Kanhaiya misl.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: SIKH WOMEN - Sikh women were known to be warriors since the time of Guru Gobind Singh, notably Mai Bhago (1670-1720), who rode into battle with forty Sikhs against Mughals in 1705. Her bravery has continued to be remembered today in the Sikh ardās, and likely inspired other women the eighteenth century like, Sada Kaur (1762-1832), who commanded the Kanhaiya Missal, after the death of her husband Gurbaksh Singh. At the age of 23 she commanded an army of 8,000 cavalrymen and fought in several battles. Her daughter was married to the young Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) and she guided her son-in-law Ranjit Singh to act in the service of the entire Sikh community and not just his own war-band, the Sukerchakia missal. This approach laid the foundation for the united Sikh Kingdom that consolidated the various war-bands under one leader in Punjab.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: REBUILDING HARIMANDAR - Sikh principalities, known as missals, emerged and collectively recaptured and rebuilt the Harmandar Sahib.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: REBUILDING HARIMANDAR – The destruction of Harimandar Sahib, later known as the Golden Temple, occurred twice by the Afghan ruler Ahmad Shah Abdali, in 1762 and 1764, levelled with gunpowder both times. After every destruction, Sikh war-bands (missals), collectively recaptured and rebuilt the temple under the leadership of Jassa Singh Ahluwalia (1718-1783). Each war-band (missal), provided funds for the construction along with building their own barracks and residences, known as a bungās, along the walkway (parkarmā). These functioned as both barracks for stationing troops, residences for missal leaders, and learning institutions for Sikh scholars. At its completion the Guru Granth Sahib was reinstalled in Harimandar Sahib in 1764, where it has been recited everyday since, without any interruption even during the military action in the premises in 1984.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: SIKHS CONQUERING PUNJAB - Over the course of twenty years, Sikh warbands (missals) conquered various parcels of land in Punjab, building Gurdwaras in memory of the Sikh Gurus.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: SIKH CONQUERING PUNJAB - From the 1760s to 1780s, Sikh military warbands, called missals, began conquering parcels of land in Punjab, largely left devastated by the invasions of Persian King Nadir Shah in 1739 and Afghan King Ahmad Shah Abdali throughout the 1760s. Out of the twelve Sikh warbands, the Bhangi missal, was one of the largest, and they took control of Lahore in 1765. The Singh Krora Missal led by Baghel Singh had a successful raid against Delhi in 1783, defeating the Mughals and conquering the Red Fort in Delhi. The Sikh forces in Delhi only left after being given land and resources to build seven Gurdwaras in Delhi connected to Sikh Gurus and notables, notably Bangla Sahib and Bala Sahib in Guru Harkrishan’s memory, Sis Ganj and Rakab Gunj in memory of Guru Tegh Bahadur, and Mata Sundri Gurdwara, Moti Bagh Gurdwara for Guru Gobind Singh, and Majnu Ka Tilla in memory of Guru Nanak.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: A LEADER EMERGES - A young Ranjit Singh, at the age of 12, takes control of the Shukarchakia Missal after his father (Maha Singh's) death.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: A LEADER EMERGES - Maharaja Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) took charge of his father’s (Maha Singh) warband, the Sukerchakia Missal, at the young age of 12. Under his leadership this war-band (missal) soon became the dominate Sikh missal in Punjab through strategic alliances and military victories. Through these efforts Ranjit Singh was crowned the Maharaja of Punjab in April of 1801. This success did not pacify Ranjit Singh’s ambition, and he continued to unite and expand Sikh forces across the Punjab and surrounding areas. Always encouraging innovation of tactics and strategy, Maharaja brought in foreign mercenaries from across Europe, including France, Hungry, Austria England, to assist with the modernization of the Khalsa army. This included implementing formation firing techniques with firearms and updating the army’s weaponry with more sophisticated cannons.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: SIKH KINGDOM - Ranjit Singh establishes a large kingdom that extended from Kabul in the west, Sutlej river in the east, Ladakh in the north and Rajasthan in the south.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: SIKH KINGDOM - The reign of Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) has been considered the golden age for Punjab. It extended westward unto Kabul, southward to Rajasthan, eastward till the Sutlej River, and northward to Ladakh. While the Great King, Maharaja, was illiterate because of being thrust into war at a young age, he greatly respected and admired knowledge and skill. Contemporary accounts note how Ranjit Singh sought out knowledgeable ministers, encouraging scientific development, and provided great amounts of patronage for schools, far surpassing the amount given during the British reign. Female education in Punjab during Ranjit Singh’s reign also shocked the British, noting it was far more prevalent in Punjab than in Europe. Ranjit Singh had ruled as a king for all, not only Sikhs, who were only around 10% of the total population

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: EQUAL JUSTICE - Akal Takht custodian Akali Phula Singh awards a penalty to Maharaja Ranjit Singh for keeping a Muslim concubine – the Maharaja graciously accepted the punishment.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: EQUAL JUSTICE - The reign of Maharaja Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) was a period of military expansionism for Punjab while at the same time stressing justice and compassion within the kingdom. Maharaja Ranjit Singh abolished the death penalty for any crime, whereas in England even petty thieves would be publicly executed. Even senior officials were not above the law. This principle of justice was displayed when the Maharaja himself was reprimanded for unethical Sikh conduct, notably marrying a Muslim courtesan named Moran. The head custodian of the Akal Takht, the Throne of the Timeless, Akali Phula Singh, summoned the Maharaja, who arrived fully willing to accept his punishment. The Akal Takht is an institution representing Sikh authority governing all Sikhs, even a Maharaja. Alongside legal reform, Ranjit Singh actively promoted education across Punjab, patronizing schools, scholars, and scientific learning, fostering a culture of knowledge that impressed contemporary observers.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: HARIMANDAR BECOMES GOLDEN - Maharaja Ranjit Singh adds gold plating to the exterior of the upper level of the Harmandir Sahib and marble to the interior and the walkways.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: HARIMANDAR BECOMES GOLDEN - Maharaja Ranjit Singh’s (1780-1839) reign in Punjab was accompanied with great prosperity and security. The Maharaja generously funded educational and religious institutions of all faiths and creeds. Most famously, during the 1830s, the Maharaja plated the Harimandar Sahib in Amritsar with gold leaf plates – this was after already decorating the Gurdwara with marble. Moreover, the Maharaja also donated the gold for the Vishwanath Mandir in Varanasi (Banaras) and the Sunehri Masjid in Lahore. Land grants records note how the Maharaja provided patronage to various religious communities equally. When commenting on his lost eye, which occurred as a result of a childhood sickness, the Maharaja claimed, “God gave me one eye to look at everyone equally.”

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: DEATH OF THE MAHARAJA - Maharaja Ranjit Singh dies and the infighting and deceit amongst his former confidants and his heirs, leads to the fall of the Sikh kingdom in 10 years.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: DEATH OF THE MAHARAJA - In 1839 the Great King, Maharaja Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) passed away at the age of 59 sparking the end of political stability in Punjab. Following his passing, Ranjit Singh’s eldest son Kharak Singh took the throne but was poisoned only a year later. Kharak Singh’s son Nau Nihal Singh then died at his father’s funeral. The infighting and chaos eventually led to Sher Singh, another claimed son of Ranjit Singh, taking the throne only to be assassinated in his second year of ruling. Eventually, Duleep Singh, Ranjit Singh’s youngest son and last sovereign king of Punjab took the throne at the young age of five and was officially deposed by the British at the age of 11 in 1849.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: EXILING THE MAHARAJA - Dalip Dingh, the son of Maharaja Ranjit Singh, deposed and exiled to London, becomes the first Sikh immigrant to England. JINDAN. BAMBA COLLECTION.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: The Anglo Sikh Wars were a series of battles, grouped within two conflicts, the First Anglo-Sikh War (1845-1846) and the Second Anglo-Sikh War (1848-1849). During this period, which saw great internal conflict within the Court of Lahore, the Khalsa Army was severally handicapped and purposefully destroyed by elites coveting power and favor from the British. Even though the Khalsa Army was betrayed by treachery from its elites, who sided with the British, the Anglo forces experienced heavy losses. Several elites within the Court of Lahore, like Raja Gulab Singh of Jammu, made secret deals with the British, which was critical for their victories. Subsequently the East India Company annexed Punjab on March 29th, 1849. It was the last Kingdom still standing in India and its subjugation led to the British rule all over the country.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: DEFINING SIKH IDENTITY - Singh Sabha is founded in Amritsar, a reform movement dedicated to consolidating Sikh traditions and preaching a distinct Sikh tradition.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: During the decades following annexation of Punjab by the British, Christian missionaries made significant grounds in converting Indians to Christianity. The new British administration revamped the education system to include English and Christianity within the curriculum, all the while defunding and destroying precolonial modes of instruction. To combat the loss of Sikh religious education, a formal society was created to promote and teach the public about the Sikh religion – this group was called the Singh Sabha. The group also fought back against the idea of an expansive Hindu religion, which subsumed Sikhism, and promoted the notion that Sikhism was distinct, ensuring Sikh rights.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: SIKH PRESS - Bhai Vir Singh opens his first press, Wazir-i-Hind, with his friend Wazir Singh. This begins literary projects like the Khalsa Tract Society (1894), and then Khalsa Samachar (1899).
timelineGalleries.longDescription: Bhai Vir Singh (1872-1957), along with his friend Wazir Singh, opened the press Wazir-i-Hind. During this time period, Bhai Vir Singh promoted the writing of Punjabi and Sikh literature, himself writing so much literature that he became known as the ‘sixth river of Punjab’, bringing a new life to Punjabi literature. British policy, which made Urdu the main governmental and instructional language in schools, was antagonistic towards Punjabi but through these efforts Punjabi remained relevant and continued to flourish, with the creation of Punjabi novels, plays, newspapers, dictionaries, and pamphlets, just to name a few. Many famous writers in the 20th century, like Puran Singh, were inspired by the efforts of Bhai Vir Singh.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: BATTLE OF SARAGARHI - Twenty-one Sikh soliders hold off tens of thousands of Afghan and Pashtun attackers, fighting to the last man heroically.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: The Battle of Saragarhi is recognized as one of the five most iconic military feats in world history. On September 12th, 1897, twenty-one Sikh soldiers were stationed at a signaling post at Saragarhi, in the North-West Frontier Province of British India, in modern Pakistan, near the Afghan border. This signalling post was stationed to give advance warning to two nearby forts. The group of Sikhs, led by Ishar Singh, were attacked by an estimated 12,000 to 24,000 Afghani soldiers. The Sikhs fought fiercely down to the last man instead of surrendering, courageously delaying the enemy forces long enough for the near by forts to prepare their defences. The entire division of Sikh soldiers were posthumously awarded the highest military award, Indian Order of Merit. The battle is a testament to Guru Gobind Singh's proverbial saying, “One Sikh is equivalent to 125,000 soldiers”.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: SIKH MARRIAGE ACT - Anand Marriage Act passed by British rulers, reinforcing the Sikh’s own identity.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: During the British colonial reign from 1849-1947, the Sikh tradition, its identity, and political rights, were constantly under threat. Sikh Reform Movements, led by the various Singh Sabha groups, endeavoured to fight for these rights, particularly legal rights in relation to the Sikh Marriage Act, which was passed in 1909. This Act allowed the Anand Kāraj ceremony to be recognized as a valid marriage ceremony. The Sikh marriage ceremony, Anand Kāraj, entails the reading of Anand Sāhib, a composition written by Guru Amardas (1479-1552), and the Lāvan, written by Guru Ramdas (1534-1581). The bride and groom circumambulate the Guru Granth Sahib four times, following each stanza of the Lāvan composition.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: SIKHS IN UK AND USA - First Gurdwara's built in UK (Putney, London) and USA (Stockton, California). Canada's first Gurdwara built in Golden, British Columbia in 1905. By 1908, nearly 5,000 Sikhs were
timelineGalleries.longDescription: In the early years of the 1900s, Sikhs arrived in UK, Canada, and America largely working in lumber mills, in North America, or factories in the UK. The earliest Gurdwara was in Canada was built in 1905, in Golden, British Columbia. In America, the first Gurdwara was built in 1912, in Stockton, California. The west coast of North America became the hub for Sikhs, who arrived on ships via Hong Kong. Many of the Sikhs arriving in North America were former military men. Due to restrictive immigration laws, Sikh women were generally not allowed to join – the Sikh workers were expected to return to South Asia after working for a number of years. Still, by the 1920, there were roughly 4-5,000 Sikhs living in North America.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: WORLD WAR 1 - 320,000 Sikhs served in World War 1 across multiple theatres of war, fighting in Flanders Field in France, across Europe, and in the Middle East.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: Sikhs contributed significantly to the British war effort during World War 1. As many as 320,000 Sikhs served across the multiple theaters of war, fighting across Europe, notably in France, as well as the Middle East, including Mesopotamia and Palestine. Sikh soldiers, specifically in the Lahore Division’s artillery, supported Canadian troops at the famous Battle of Vimy Ridge. Sikh troops also participated in the 2nd Ypres and St Julian Battle, the Battle of Festubert, and Somme. Over 15,000 Sikhs gave their lives in World War 1. Sikhs were only 2% of the population of British India but represented nearly 22% of their total fighting force and were one of highest decorated groups, winning over 14 Victoria Crosses for extraordinary bravery.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: AMRITSAR MASSACRE - Jallianwala Bagh massacre in Amritsar. British General Dyer ordered troops to fire at 20,000 protestors gathered to peacefully protest the Rowlatt Act. Hundreds were killed and ove
timelineGalleries.longDescription: Following World War 1, independence movements across India grew rapidly. To counter the growing resistance, the British created the Rowlatt Act, which granted police the ability to detain and arrest protestors without granting them the right to a trial. To protest this draconian law, Sikhs gathered at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar on Vaisakhi, April 13th, 1919. Large crowds had gathered because they were also celebrating Vaisakhi. The British commander, Reginald Dyer, viewing the large protesting crowd as a threat to British rule, blocked the only exit and fired upon the crowd without warning. Hundreds were killed and over a thousand wounded during the massacre. The brutality of the act only emboldened anticolonial efforts, marking a turning point for nationalist resistance. Some estimates tally the death toll to over 1,000 souls, with nearly 4,000 injured.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: GURDWARA MANAGEMENT - Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhic Committee (SGPC) and the Akali Party are founded to free Gurdwara's of corrupt practices.SIKH GURDWARA BILL -1925- The bill is passed and it confers
timelineGalleries.longDescription: The Shiromani Gurdwara Prabandhak Committee (SGPC), an organization which manages and administers control over Sikh Gurdwaras, was established in 1920. For many years, during the Sikh Reform Movement, Sikhs fought for the right to control and manage important Sikh shrines. The Jaito Morcha, in 1924, was a non-violent protest demanding Sikh religious rights but was met with cruelty, and many Sikhs lost their lives at the hands of British soldiers. These sacrifices paved the way for the Sikh Gurdwara Act of 1925, which codified into law that only an elected body of orthodox Sikhs could manage Gurdwaras. The bill codified the general will of the Sikh community, providing legal support to the SGPC.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: BHAGAT SINGH.   - Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev and Rajguru were accused of murder of an English police officer and were executed in Lahore. Their slogans ‘Inquilab zindabad’ and ‘Down with imperialism’ are t
timelineGalleries.longDescription: During the 1920s, anticolonial resistance expanded greatly in India. Following the death of an important resistance leader, Lala Lajpat Rai in 1928, as a result of a police lathi charge, Bhagat Singh and his accomplice, Shivaram Rajguru, carried out an act of retaliation, killing a police office in Lahore. Bhagat Singh was inspired by the Ghadar Party, an anticolonial group based along the west coast of North America, along with ideals exposed by Communist writers like Marx, Lenin and Trotsky. Bhagat Singh along with his accomplices were charged and hung in Lahore on March 23rd, 1931. The cries of “Down with Imperialism” and "Inquilab Zindabad",  which Bhagat Singh recited during his trial, inspired and reenergized the anticolonial movement, which fought fiercely to achieve independence in 1947.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: WORLD WAR 2 - Over 300,000 Sikhs served in World War 2 - fighting for the Allied Forces in Europe, Africa and South East Asia. The awards won by Sikhs pay testament to their courage in the battlefield
timelineGalleries.longDescription: Sikh soldier participation in World War 2, under the Allied Forces, numbered over 300,000, playing a crucial role in the various theaters of war across Europe, North Africa, and Southeast Asia. During this service, five British Sikhs won the most prestigious award, the Victoria Cross for their bravery and courage in battle. Many other awards for their service were also bestowed on Sikh soldiers. While Sikhs only comprised 2% of British India’s population, they served upwards of 20% of the entire British Indian force. There were also Canadian and American Sikhs who served under the Canadian & American armies during World War 2. Nearly 90,000 Indian troops died in World War 2, largely of Sikh background.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: JUSTICE FOR THE INNOCENT - Udham Singh shoots dead Sir Michel O’Dwyer, in London, who was the Lt. Governor of Punjab at the time of the Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: Nearly twenty years after the Jallianwala Bagh massacre in Amritsar, occurring in 1919, Udham Singh shot and killed Sir Michael O’Dwyer in London on March 13th, 1940. O’Dwyer was the acting Lieutenant Governor of Punjab when the Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred, not to be confused with Reginald Dyer, the commander who ordered the massacre. Udham Singh was motivated to bring to justice those who were responsible for the oppression occurring in British India and he was later hanged later that year in July 1940. Udham Singh took the name Ram Mohammad Singh Azad while in prison, reflecting both his anticolonial mindset, Azad meaning freedom, and his united vision reflecting the three major religious traditions in India – Hindu, Sikh, and Muslim.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: Revolutionary Army – Subhas Chandra Bose, an anti-colonial leader from Orissa, organized a largely Sikh force of WWII POWs released by the Japanese to fight the British.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: While Sikh soldiers fought in great numbers for the British during World War 2, at the same time many anticolonial resistance efforts were still taking place. Subhas Chandra Bose was a prominent anticolonial leader, who encouraged armed resistance against the British. Bose created ties with Japan, who were fighting the British, convincing them to transfer their captured Indian soldiers, many of which were Sikh soldiers, so they could fight against the British under a newly organized, ‘Indian National Army’ battalion. Bose also traveled to Germany to establish ties with the Nazi Party, in efforts to undermine the British in India. Although the INA was not successful, its efforts encouraged resistance fighters and thinkers in India to rise up against the British.

timelineGalleries.shortDescription: INDEPENDENCE, PARTITION.   India gains independence from the British and Punjab in partitioned, Hindus and Sikhs flee to India and Muslims to Pakistan, with enormous loss of life and property.
timelineGalleries.longDescription: India gained its independence in 1947 when the Indian subcontinent was divided into two nations, India and Pakistan. The borders of the division, largely based on religious lines, were created by British administers who were unaware of the circumstances on the ground. During the process of independence, communal violence erupted across the region. Hindus and Sikhs were forced to flee the region of Pakistan for India and Muslims left India for Pakistan. This resulted in the greatest mass migration in human history, resulting in upwards of a million people killed during the violence. Despite advocating for a separate state, Sikhs were subsumed under India, even though they proportionally sacrificed more for the freedom movement than other groups.

data.name: Sikh Galleries
data.description: An immersive, educational journey that inspires curiosity—empowering you to discover, explore, and learn seamlessly as you engage with the collection.

galleries.title: Birth of baby Nanak
galleries.shortDescription: This Circa 1910 painting titled "Birth of Nanak" by Sardul Singh, Amritsar, India, uses pen and ink and wash on paper.
galleries.longDescription: In the village of Talwandi, in Punjab, the birth of Guru Nanak was remembered as an extraordinary moment. Pre colonial artistic depictions often show the infant Guru in the lap of his mother, Mata Tripta, surrounded by villagers and Indic deities in reverence. The depiction of mythic figures was used to convey the immense significance of Guru Nanak’s arrival. Their presence artistically represents that even celestial beings come to pay homage, expressing how deeply revered the Guru was. From a young age, Nanak’s actions and words carried profound wisdom, notably his compassion, wisdom, and willingness to question empty ritual inspired those around him. Even as a child, people sensed that his life would become a source of guidance, illuminating a path toward truth and the recognition of the Divine in all.

galleries.title: God is ONE
galleries.shortDescription: This Circa 1982 painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji is done in the Punjab Hills, India, is attributed to Sobha Singh and uses Oil on Board.
galleries.longDescription: At age 27, Guru Nanak disappeared while bathing in the Kali Bein River. Believing he had drowned, people searched for him but found nothing. After three days, he reappeared, having entered a profound state of meditation. When he finally spoke, he declared: “There is no Hindu, there is no Muslim.” In a Punjab marked by tension between these communities, the Guru’s words challenged rigid religious identities. He taught that people should look beyond labels and focus instead on virtuous living and finding the Divine within. For Guru Nanak, God is formless, boundless, and the creator and sustainer of the universe. This vision of the One Divine—Ik Onkār—was inseparable from his teaching on human unity: all share the same divine light, and devotion is expressed through humility and service to others.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI ON UDASSI
galleries.shortDescription: This 21 century large painting done by Arpana Caur in Delhi, India, uses Oil on canvas and is 69 x 54 inches.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Nanak’s thirst for exploration is evidenced by their travels, estimated at covering nearly 20,000 miles. Throughout these journeys, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana encountered people from many communities and walks of life. The Sikh writer Bhai Gurdas observed, “It was apparent in the world—Baba was the Pir for the Muslims and Guru for the Hindus”. This meant, regardless of one’s background, they felt they could learn from Guru Nanak. Regardless of how people treated Guru Nanak on his travels, his actions and poetry became instructive. He consistently taught about the oneness of Divinity and the unity of humanity, reminding people that beyond all social divisions and religious labels, all people shared the same divine light.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK AT MECCA
galleries.shortDescription: This mid 19th century painting depicts Guru Nanak Ji in Mecca, is done in Punjab, India, uses gouache paint on paper.
galleries.longDescription: During Guru Nanak's (1469-1539) travels he spread his divinely inspired message of unity and devotion. Covering incredible distances on foot, the Guru with his Muslim devotee Mardana reached the city of Mecca, the holiest of cities for the Islamic tradition. Guru Nanak was described wearing blue on these travels, symbolically important for Sufi Muslims, while also wearing items that signify his connection to Indic traditions – signifying his transcendence over identity. Upon reaching the Ka'ba, the central temple in the city, the Guru rested, pointing his feet at the holy temple. This angered a local priest, who then grabbed Nanak’s feet. The Guru replied, “Point these feet where there is no God” – the shocked priest realized his folly, understanding the Guru’s lesson that God is omnipresent, present within the entire world and beyond.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI WITH MARDANA AND BALA
galleries.shortDescription: This 19th century painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji done in Tanjore, India, uses gouache paint with gold and semiprecious stones on rice paper glued on teak wood.
galleries.longDescription: As a young boy, Guru Nanak was brought to undergo the janeyū ceremony, a sacred thread ritual marking entry into religious maturity for certain castes. Before accepting it, the young Nanak questioned the priests about its true spiritual value. He asked whether righteousness came from merely wearing a thread or from living a virtuous life. In response, Nanak described a different kind of sacred thread: one made of compassion, contentment, self-restraint, and truth. Such a thread, he explained, cannot break, burn, or wear away, unlike the physical one. Through this teaching, Nanak emphasized that inner virtues and righteous actions—not outward symbols or caste rituals—define true spirituality. This principle later became foundational to Sikh teachings, where devotion is expressed through ethical living, humility, and service to others.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI WITH BHAI MARDANA AND BALA
galleries.shortDescription: This circa 1990 painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji, done by contemporary artist Bodhraj in Punjab, India, uses Oil on canvas.
galleries.longDescription: A central principle in Sikh thought is hard and honest work, known as Kirat Kamai. Guru Nanak emphasized that spiritual life is not separate from everyday labor; rather, work done with integrity becomes a form of devotion. After his travels, Guru Nanak settled in Kartarpur, where he lived as a farmer, cultivating the land alongside the community. The harvest from this labor was not kept for personal gain but shared with others, especially those in need. Within the Guru’s writings he also proclaimed this teaching, “Those, who eat from their hard-earned money, while providing for others; such people know the path” (Guru Granth: 1245). In this way Guru Nanak demonstrated through his actions and words how generosity and service were key to Sikh practice and necessary for spiritual realization.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI WITH LANGAR SCENE
galleries.shortDescription: This 20th century painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji, done in Punjab, India, uses gouache paint heightened with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: From a young age, Guru Nanak saw the Divine not only in the formless Absolute but in every human being. When his father entrusted him with twenty rupees to make a profit, instead of buying supplies to sell, the young Nanak provided food and clothing to ascetics in need. When explaining his reasoning to his father, Guru Nanak argued that charity was in fact the true investment – which returned greater value than anything possible from ordinary commerce. This act, remembered as Sachā Saudā (the True Bargain), crystallized a principle that would shape the Sikh tradition: Langar, the communal kitchen, where all—regardless of caste, creed, or status—sit together and eat. For Guru Nanak, service to humanity was acting out the principle that the Divine is within, and honoured, in everyone.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI IN YELLOW INSCRIBED ROBE
galleries.shortDescription: This 19th century painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji is done in Lucknow or Deccan, India, and uses opaque pigment on paper with floral borders.
galleries.longDescription: A principle reflected in Sikh tradition is that devotion to the Divine is not bound to a single language. Guru Nanak composed and spoke in multiple tongues, Punjabi, Hindi, Persian, and regional dialects, so that people of various backgrounds could understand and reflect on the Divine. In Sikh thought, the sacred message of devotion can be expressed through any language that reaches the heart. Artistic depictions represent the perspective of the painter, as such, sometimes Guru Nanak is depicted in Islamic attire, while at other times wearing Indic symbols. Sikh scripture, although for a Sikh audience is written in Gurmukhi script, has been written in Arabic and Persian to reach broader audiences. Guru Nanak here is wearing a robe with Arabic inscription, which is preserved at Dera Baba Nanak, in Punjab.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI
galleries.shortDescription: This mid-late 19th century painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji done in the Punjab plains, India, uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Depictions of Guru Nanak range from Islamic to Indic style, representing how various people of faith understood and viewed Guru Nanak. Many pre colonial depictions, mirroring early historical texts, display the Guru wearing elements from both the Islamic and Indic traditions, which represents a transcendence of identity – that the Guru is neither yet connected to both communities. The cap (selī topī), an indicator of Islamic practice, is balanced by the Hindu sacred thread (janeū) worn across the torso and the forehead mark (tilak). The halo around Guru Nanak’s head signifies divine authority, from the Persian tradition, while the ornate earrings represent participation within the world, as opposed to earrings worn by renunciate yogis. Taken all together, these symbols convey a key message of Guru Nanak’s teachings: spiritual truth transcends religious boundaries and that the Divine light shines equally within all communities.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK DEV JI
galleries.shortDescription: This circa 1840 painting of Guru Nanak Dev Ji is done in Deccan, India, using gouache paint highlighted with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: There are no paintings of Guru Nanak from his lifetime. The images that were produced thereafter, even some during the lifetimes of the successor Gurus, depict the Guru in various ways, depending on the background and style of the painter. While many early depictions display the Guru wearing symbols from both the Islamic and Indic community, representing a transcendence of identity, rarely one finds a painting depicting the Guru from a singular perspective. Here, Guru Nanak is wearing the Islamic teal attire with a short conical cap (selī ṭopī). Guru Nanak’s head is framed by a luminescent halo, signifying divine authority, with a flying winged angel (farishta), descending from the heavens, who is showering blessings. The nasta’liq inscription reads, “The First Master”.

trailers.description: A serene gallery honoring Guru Nanak Dev Ji, showcasing his life, teachings, and spiritual journey—spreading messages of unity, compassion, and equality that continue to inspire humanity across generations.

galleries.title: TEN SIKH GURUS
galleries.shortDescription: This 19 century painting of the Ten Guru’s done in Tanjore uses gouache and gold and semi precious stones on rice paper on teak wood.
galleries.longDescription: The Sikh tradition began in Punjab, India, with Guru Nanak (1469–1539), whose poetic revelation of divine unity—“Ik Oankar”, there is One Divine, merged within everything, formed the foundation of Sikhi. His teachings drew seekers (Sikhs, meaning students) and established a lineage of Gurus who carried and shaped this message. Each successor preserved its core while guiding the community through new historical realities, culminating with Guru Gobind Singh, who vested authority in the Guru Granth Sahib as the eternal Guru. Organized in rāgs, the scripture speaks of divine unity realized through sung devotion and ethical practice. The Gurus instructed Sikhs to live as engaged householders, practice equality, and serve humanity through langar (degh). After martyrdom and persecution, the tradition embraced the responsibility to defend justice (tegh), crystallizing in the formation of the Khalsa in 1699.

galleries.title: GURU NANAK JI (1469-1539)
galleries.shortDescription: This circa 1830 painting of Guru Nanak Ji is done in the Punjab plains and uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Nanak (1469–1539), the first Guru, displayed spiritual depth from early childhood, recognized especially by his elder sister Bebe Nanaki. Immersed in divine awareness, he rejected social divisions of caste, creed, gender, and religion, seeing the Divine equally in all. After a transformative experience in the River Kali Bein, he proclaimed, “There is no Hindu or Muslim,” teaching that beneath outward identities lies one divine unity. Traveling widely (estimated at over 17,000 miles) with his companion Mardana, who played the rabāb, Guru Nanak spread this message through song across South Asia and beyond, challenging hypocrisy and affirming women’s equality. Rejecting renunciation, he taught that spiritual realization was obtained within society. At Kartarpur, he established a farming community and free kitchen (langar), grounding devotion in honest labor, charity, and remembrance of the Divine.

galleries.title: GURU ANGAD JI (1504-1552)
galleries.shortDescription: This 19th century painting of Guru Angad Ji is done in North India and uses gouache paint accented by gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Angad (1504–1552), the second Guru, had a life-changing moment when meeting Guru Nanak in his late 20s. Deeply transformed, he served Guru Nanak devotedly and, after passing spiritual tests, was named “Angad” (limb), symbolizing unity with Nanak’s spirit, a foundational Sikh doctrine. He systematized and promoted the Gurmukhi script, compiled Guru Nanak’s hymns, and composed sixty-two verses preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib, adopting the pen name “Nanak” to express continuity of authority. Alongside Mata Khivi, his wife, he strengthened the institution of langar, modeling equality by sitting and eating with all. He encouraged physical discipline through wrestling arenas and supported the creation of early Sikh historical texts. Before his passing, he appointed Guru Amardas as his successor, ensuring the continuity of the Guru’s mission.

galleries.title: GURU AMARDAS JI (1479-1574)
galleries.shortDescription: This circa 1840 painting of Guru Amardas Ji is done in the Punjab plains and uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Amardas (1479–1574), the third Guru, spent much of his early life searching for spiritual fulfilment. He found his path after hearing Bibi Amaro sing Guru Nanak’s hymns, which led him to serve Guru Angad for nearly two decades. Appointed Guru at the late age of seventy-three, he energetically expanded and organized the Sikh community. He founded the town of Goindwal and commissioned the Baoli Sahib step well as both a practical and pilgrimage site. Guru Amardas institutionalised major gatherings at Vaisakhi and Diwali and appointed twenty-two regional leaders (manjis) to spread the message. A prolific poet, he composed 907 verses, including the Anand Sahib, and helped preserve earlier hymns that later formed part of the Adi Granth. He appointed Guru Ramdas as his successor, ensuring continuity of leadership and vision.

galleries.title: GURU RAMDAS JI (1534-1581)
galleries.shortDescription: This early 19th century painting of Guru Ramdas Ji is done in Rajasthan and uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Ramdas (1534–1581), the fourth Guru and son-in-law of Guru Amardas, was born in Lahore into the Sodhi clan. From a young age he served Guru Amardas and was entrusted with representing Sikh affairs in legal and religious matters. Land grants from the Mughal Emperor Akbar were placed in his name, enabling him to settle the city of Ramdaspur, later known as Amritsar, which became the spiritual center of the Sikh community. A prolific poet, he composed 679 verses preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib, including the Lāvān hymns recited at Sikh weddings and portions of the Rehrās prayer. Skilled in musicology, he composed in thirty rāgs. Before his passing at age forty-seven, he appointed his youngest son, Guru Arjan, as successor, ensuring continuity of leadership.

galleries.title: GURU ARJAN JI (1563-1606)
galleries.shortDescription: Guru Arjan Dev seated in a serene setting, symbolizing devotion, sacrifice, and spiritual authority.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Arjan Dev, the fifth Guru, is depicted seated on a golden platform with a rosary, accompanied by an attendant. The vibrant setting with flowers and clouds creates a calm yet divine atmosphere. The painting reflects his spiritual authority and contributions, including compiling the Adi Granth and promoting harmony, devotion, and sacrifice.

galleries.title: GURU HARGOBIND JI (1595-1644)
galleries.shortDescription: This circa 1838 painting of Guru Hargobind Ji is done in Punjab and uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Hargobind (1595–1644), the sixth Guru and son of Guru Arjan, led the community after his father’s martyrdom, marking a decisive shift toward martial preparedness. Trained in both scripture and arms by Baba Budha, the most senior Sikh, he embodied the doctrine of mīrī pīrī – sovereignty and spirituality – symbolized by the two swords adorned on his waistband. He constructed the Akal Takht in Amritsar as the seat of Sikh political and military power and maintained a standing army. Imprisoned by the Mughal Emperor Jahangir, he secured release only after ensuring freedom for fifty-two other political prisoners. Guru Hargobind fought four defensive battles against Mughal forces and founded towns such as Hargobindpur and Kiratpur. Before his passing in 1644, he appointed his grandson Guru Har Rai as successor, firmly establishing the Sikh martial tradition.

galleries.title: GURU HAR RAI JI (1630-1661)
galleries.shortDescription: This 20th century painting of Guru Har Rai Ji is done in Punjab and uses gouache paint accented by gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Har Rai (1630–1661), the seventh Guru and grandson of Guru Hargobind, was appointed at age fifteen and led the community for seventeen years from Kiratpur. While maintaining the martial legacy of mīrī and pīrī, he sustained a cavalry of 2,200 soldiers yet avoided direct conflict. Deeply compassionate, he spent time in forest retreats, fostering devotion through kirtan and discourse, and established a sanctuary for animals. Trained in Ayurveda, he famously provided medicine to the Mughal prince Dara Shikoh, forming a respectful relationship amid shifting imperial politics. Guru Har Rai navigated both external tensions and internal challenges while keeping the community centered on Gurbani and the teachings of Guru Nanak. Before his passing, he appointed his younger son, Guru Har Krishan, as successor.

galleries.title: GURU HAR KISHAN JI (1656-1664)
galleries.shortDescription: This circa 1880 painting of Guru Har Kishan Ji is done in the Punjab plains and uses gouache paint accented generously with gold.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Har Krishan (1656–1664), the eighth Guru and youngest son of Guru Har Rai, was appointed at age five and led the community with remarkable wisdom and humility. Though a child, he met influential Rajput rulers and won their admiration. Sikh tradition remembers him for illuminating divine wisdom in others and for his compassionate service during a devastating smallpox epidemic. Refusing to align with Mughal political interference despite the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb’s summons, he remained devoted to aiding the suffering. In serving the afflicted, he contracted smallpox and passed away in Delhi at age eight. Before his death, he uttered “Baba Bakala,” indicating that his successor, his elder (Baba) would be found in Bakala, Guru Tegh Bahadur. He is remembered as the compassionate child Guru whose presence brought healing and solace.

galleries.title: GURU TEG BAHADUR JI            (1621-1665)
galleries.shortDescription: This 18th century painting of Guru Teg Bahadur Ji is done in Rajasthan and uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Tegh Bahadur (1621–1675), the ninth Guru and son of Guru Hargobind, combined martial skill with profound spiritual detachment. Trained in arms and seasoned in battle, he nevertheless was known for his embodied deep meditation and contentment, composing 116 hymns later incorporated into the Guru Granth Sahib. After being identified as Guru in 1664, he traveled widely across Punjab and eastern India, strengthening the community and supporting the needy. When Kashmiri Pandits sought protection from forced conversions under Emperor Aurangzeb, Guru Tegh Bahadur defended their right to religious freedom. Arrested and tortured in Delhi, he refused to convert to Islam and was executed in 1675. His martyrdom, remembered as unparalleled, upheld the principle of freedom of conscience and paved the path for his son, Guru Gobind Singh.

galleries.title: GURU GOBIND SINGH JI (1666-1708)
galleries.shortDescription: This mid 19th century painting of Guru Gobind Singh Ji is done in the Punjab plains and uses gouache paint accented with gold on paper.
galleries.longDescription: Guru Gobind Singh (1666–1708), the tenth Guru and son of Guru Tegh Bahadur, was born in Patna and raised in Anandpur. Exceptionally educated in Gurmukhi, Persian, and Sanskrit, he combined literary brilliance with a rigorous martial mindset. A gifted poet and patron of the arts, he infused his compositions with a heroic ethos – his writings are located within the Dasam Granth. Amid mounting conflict, he reshaped the Sikh community into a disciplined martial order. In 1699, at Vaisakhi, he founded the Khalsa, initiating the Five Beloved Ones and instituting the Five Ks as symbols of commitment and sovereignty. Following intense battles with Hill chiefs and the Mughals, he finalized the Guru Granth Sahib by adding Guru Tegh Bahadur’s hymns. Before his death in 1708, he vested eternal Guruship in the Guru Granth Sahib.

trailers.description: A sacred gallery honoring the Ten Sikh Gurus, tracing their teachings, legacy, and spiritual lineage—celebrating values of unity, equality, courage, and devotion that continue to guide humanity.
